In April 2014, she was named on Hay Festival's Africa39 list of 39 sub-Saharan African writers aged under 40 with the potential and talent to define trends in African literature. Her short story "The Sack" won the 2015 Caine Prize for African fiction in English. In 2020, Serpell won the Belles-lettres category Grand Prix of Literary Associations 2019 for her debut novel The Old Drift.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Slowking4 · GFDL 1.2 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Laura Jean McKay
authors.bio: Laura Jean McKay is an Australian author and creative writing lecturer. In 2021, she won the Victorian Prize for Literature and the Arthur C. Clarke Award for her novel The Animals in That Country. In 2025, she was the Frank O'Connor International Fellow.

authors.name: Harry Josephine Giles
authors.bio: Harry Josephine Giles is a Scottish writer, singer and poet who previously lived in Orkney. In 2022, she won the Arthur C. Clarke Award for their novel Deep Wheel Orcadia.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by byronv2 · CC BY 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Ned Beauman
authors.bio: Ned Beauman is a British novelist, journalist and screenwriter. The author of five novels, he was selected as one of the Best of Young British Novelists by Granta magazine in 2013.

authors.name: Martin MacInnes
authors.bio: Martin MacInnes is a Scottish author of three novels including Infinite Ground (2016), which won the Somerset Maugham Award, and In Ascension (2023). His work often delves into themes of the human condition in the 21st century, weaving narratives that reflect on the tensions between digital advancement and ecological devastation.

authors.name: Sierra Greer
authors.bio: Caragh M. O'Brien is an American novelist, best known as the author of the Birthmarked trilogy, a series of young adult, dystopian novels. Her books take place in a world destroyed by climate change. She also wrote an adult novel, Annie Bot, about a malfunctioning gynoid companion, under the pen name Sierra Greer.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Jeffrey Beall · CC BY 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Bob Shaw
authors.bio: Robert Shaw was a science fiction writer and fan from Northern Ireland, noted for his originality and wit. He won the Hugo Award for Best Fan Writer in 1979 and 1980. His short story "Light of Other Days" was a Hugo Award nominee in 1967, as was his novel The Ragged Astronauts in 1987.

authors.name: Michael G. Coney
authors.bio: Michael Greatrex Coney was a British science fiction writer, best known for his novel Hello Summer, Goodbye.

authors.name: Ian Watson
authors.bio: Ian Watson was a British science fiction writer who lived in Gijón, Spain.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Salvatore Capalbi from Italy · CC BY-SA 2.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: J. G. Ballard
authors.bio: James Graham Ballard was an English novelist and short-story writer, satirist and essayist known for psychologically provocative works of fiction that explore the relations between human psychology, technology, sex and mass media. Ballard first became associated with New Wave science fiction for post-apocalyptic novels such as The Drowned World (1962).

authors.name: John Sladek
authors.bio: John Thomas Sladek was an American science fiction author, known for his satirical and surreal novels.

authors.name: Robert Holdstock
authors.bio: Robert Paul Holdstock was an English novelist and author best known for his works of Celtic, Nordic, Gothic and Pictish fantasy literature, predominantly in the fantasy subgenre of mythic fiction.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Philippe Gindre · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Keith Roberts
authors.bio: Keith John Kingston Roberts was an English science fiction author. He began publishing with two stories in the September 1964 issue of Science Fantasy magazine, "Anita" and "Escapism".

authors.name: Christopher Evans
authors.bio: Christopher D. Evans is a British science fiction writer and children's author. His novels include Capella's Golden Eyes (1980); The Insider (1981); Mortal Remains (1995); and Ice Tower (2000). He is the co-editor of three original SF anthologies, Other Edens (1987); Other Edens II (1988); and Other Edens III (1989).

authors.name: Iain M. Banks
authors.bio: Iain Menzies Banks was a Scottish author, writing mainstream fiction as Iain Banks and science fiction as Iain M. Banks. His books have been adapted for theatre, radio, and television. In 2008, The Times named Banks in their list of the 50 greatest British writers since 1945.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by TimDuncan · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Mary Gentle
authors.bio: Mary Rosalyn Gentle is a British science fiction and fantasy author.

authors.name: Alastair Reynolds
authors.bio: Alastair Preston Reynolds is a Welsh science fiction author. He specialises in hard science fiction and space opera.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Robert Day · CC BY-SA 2.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Jon Courtenay Grimwood
authors.bio: Jon Courtenay Grimwood is a Maltese-born British science fiction and fantasy author. He also writes literary fiction as Jonathan Grimwood, and crime fiction and thrillers as Jack Grimwood.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Sam Baker · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Gareth L. Powell
authors.bio: Gareth Lyn Powell is a British author of science fiction. His works include the Embers of War trilogy, the Continuance series, the Ack-Ack Macaque trilogy, Light Chaser, and About Writing, a guide for aspiring authors. He has also co-written stories with authors Peter F. Hamilton and Aliette de Bodard.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by JendiaGammon · CC BY 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Aliette de Bodard
authors.bio: Aliette de Bodard is a French-American speculative fiction writer. She has received accolades including the British Fantasy Award, BSFA Award, Ignyte Award, Locus Award, and Nebula Award.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Aliette de Bodard (subject and creator) · CC BY 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Dave Hutchinson
authors.bio: Dave Hutchinson is a science fiction writer who was born in Sheffield in England in 1960 and read American Studies at the University of Nottingham. He subsequently moved into journalism, writing for The Weekly News and The Courier for almost 25 years.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Cecilia Weightman · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Nina Allan
authors.bio: Nina Allan is a British writer of speculative fiction. She has published five collections of short stories, multiple novella-sized works, and five novels. Her stories have appeared in the magazines Interzone, Black Static and Crimewave and have been nominated for or won a number of awards, including the Grand prix de l'Imaginaire and the BSFA Award.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by S.tollyfield · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Juliet E. McKenna
authors.bio: Juliet E. McKenna is a British fantasy author. Her novels mostly form part of series, five series as of 2022.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Johan Jönsson (Julle) · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Aliya Whiteley
authors.bio: Aliya Whiteley is a British novelist, short story writer and poet.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by DavidPMaynard · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: E.J. Swift
authors.bio: Taylor Alison Swift is an American singer-songwriter. An influential figure in popular culture, she is known for her autobiographical songwriting and artistic reinventions. Swift is the highest-grossing live music artist, the wealthiest female musician, and one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by iHeartRadioCA · CC BY 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Phyllis Gotlieb
authors.bio: Phyllis Fay Gotlieb was a Canadian science fiction novelist and poet.

authors.name: Eileen Kernaghan
authors.bio: Eileen Kernaghan is a Canadian novelist, poet, and four-time winner of the Prix Aurora Award for English-language Canadian speculative fiction. The settings of her historical fantasy novels range from the prehistoric Indus Valley and eighteenth century Bhutan, to Elizabethan England and nineteenth century Scandinavia. She lives on Saltspring Island, British Columbia.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Scruffygarden · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Guy Gavriel Kay
authors.bio: Guy Gavriel Kay is a Canadian writer of fantasy fiction. The majority of his novels take place in fictional settings that resemble real places during real historical periods, such as Constantinople during the reign of Justinian I or Spain during the time of El Cid. Kay has expressed a preference to avoid genre categorization of these works as historical fantasy. As of 2025, Kay has published 16 novels and a book of poetry.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Laura Kay, photographer · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Charles de Lint
authors.bio: Charles de Lint is a Canadian writer.

authors.name: Dave Duncan
authors.bio: David John Duncan was a Canadian fantasy and science fiction author.

authors.name: Sean Stewart
authors.bio: Sean Stewart is the pseudonym of Michael Sean Irwin, an American-Canadian science fiction and fantasy author.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by DingoDeFalco · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Candas Jane Dorsey
authors.bio: Candas Jane Dorsey is a Canadian poet and science fiction novelist who resides in her hometown of Edmonton, Alberta.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Edgar Zessinthal from Quebec, Canada · CC BY 2.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Julie E. Czerneda
authors.bio: Julie E. Czerneda is a Canadian science fiction and fantasy author. She has written many novels, including four Aurora Award for Best Novel winners, and a number of short stories; she has also edited several anthologies.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Brashley46 · CC BY 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Karl Schroeder
authors.bio: Karl Schroeder is a Canadian science fiction author and futurist. His novels present far-future speculations on topics such as nanotechnology, terraforming, augmented reality, and interstellar travel, and are deeply philosophical. More recently he also focuses on near-future topics. Several of his short stories feature the character Gennady Malianov.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Dave Hogg · CC BY 2.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Edo van Belkom
authors.bio: Edo van Belkom is a Canadian author of horror fiction.

authors.name: Karin Lowachee
authors.bio: Karin Lowachee is a Canadian author of speculative fiction. She is best known for her Warchild series, including Warchild (2002), Burndive (2003), and Cagebird (2005).

authors.name: Nalo Hopkinson
authors.bio: Nalo Hopkinson is a Jamaican-born Canadian speculative fiction writer and editor. Her novels – Brown Girl in the Ring (1998), Midnight Robber (2000), The Salt Roads (2003), The New Moon's Arms (2007) – and short stories such as those in her collection Skin Folk (2001) often draw on Caribbean history and language, and its traditions of oral and written storytelling.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Markku Lappalainen · CC BY 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Edward Willett
authors.bio: Edward Willett is a US/Canadian writer of speculative fiction, predominately known for the Right to Know, Shapers of Worlds, Shards of Excalibur, The Helix War, The Masks of Aygrima, and Worldshapers fiction series. Has written under the pseudonyms Adam Blade, E. C. Blake, and Lee Arthur Chane.

authors.name: Tanya Huff
authors.bio: Tanya Sue Huff is a Canadian fantasy author. Her stories have been published since the late 1980s, including five fantasy series and one science fiction series. One of these, her Blood Books series, featuring detective Vicki Nelson, was adapted for television under the title Blood Ties.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Michael Pereckas · CC BY-SA 2.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: A. M. Dellamonica
authors.bio: Alyxandra Margaret "A. M." Dellamonica is a Canadian speculative fiction writer who has published over forty short stories in the field since the 1980s. Dellamonica writes in a number of subgenres including science fiction, fantasy, and alternate history. Their stories have been selected for "Year's Best" science fiction anthologies in 2002 and 2007. Dellamonica is non-binary.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Tabercil · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Fonda Lee
authors.bio: Fonda Lee is a Canadian-American author of speculative fiction. She is best known for writing The Green Bone Saga, the first of which, Jade City, won the 2018 World Fantasy Award and was named one of the 100 Best Fantasy Books of All Time by Time magazine. The Green Bone Saga was also included on NPR's list, "50 Favorite Sci-Fi and Fantasy Books of the Past Decade".
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Gage Skidmore · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Kate Heartfield
authors.bio: Kate Heartfield is a Canadian author of fantasy, science fiction, horror, as well as a non-fiction writer and editor.

authors.name: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
authors.bio: Silvia Moreno-Garcia is a Mexican-Canadian novelist, short story writer, editor, and publisher.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Rosiestep · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Premee Mohamed
authors.bio: Premee Mohamed is an Indo-Caribbean scientist and speculative fiction author based in Edmonton, Alberta. She also works as Social Media Manager and Associate Editor for Escape Pod.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by librairie mollat · CC BY 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Scott Westerfeld
authors.bio: Scott David Westerfeld is an American writer of young adult fiction, best known as the author of the Uglies and the Leviathan series.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Ceridwen · CC BY 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Mira Grant
authors.bio: Seanan McGuire is an American author and filker. McGuire is known for her urban fantasy novels. She uses the pseudonym Mira Grant to write science fiction/horror and the pseudonym A. Deborah Baker to write the "Up-and-Under" children's portal fantasy series.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Library of Congress Life · CC0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Stephen King
authors.bio: Stephen Edwin King is an American author. Dubbed the "King of Horror", he is widely known for his horror fiction and has also explored other genres, among them suspense, crime, science-fiction, fantasy, and mystery. He has written approximately 200 short stories, most of which have been published in collections.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Kevin Payravi · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Andy Weir
authors.bio: Andy Weir is an American novelist. His 2011 novel The Martian was adapted into the 2015 film of the same name. He received the John W. Campbell Award for Best New Writer in 2016. His 2021 novel Project Hail Mary was a finalist for the 2022 Hugo Award for Best Novel, and was adapted into the 2026 film of the same name.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Gage Skidmore · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Pierce Brown
authors.bio: Pierce Brown is an American science fiction author who writes the Red Rising series, consisting of Red Rising (2014), Golden Son (2015), Morning Star (2016), Iron Gold (2018), Dark Age (2019), and Light Bringer (2023). He also has written a six-issue prequel comic book series, Red Rising: Sons of Ares, that was published in 2015.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Gage Skidmore · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: V.E. Schwab
authors.bio: Victoria Elizabeth Schwab is an American writer. She is known for the 2013 novel Vicious, the Shades of Magic series, and The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue, which was nominated for the 2020 Locus Award for Best Fantasy Novel. She publishes children's and young adult fiction books under the name Victoria Schwab.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Bryan Berlin · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Blake Crouch
authors.bio: William Blake Crouch is an American author known for books such as Dark Matter, Recursion, Upgrade, and his Wayward Pines Trilogy, which was adapted into a television series in 2015. Dark Matter was adapted for television in 2024.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Gage Skidmore · CC BY-SA 3.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Christopher Paolini
authors.bio: Christopher James Paolini is an American and Italian author. He is best known for The Inheritance Cycle, which consists of the books Eragon (2002), Eldest (2005), Brisingr (2008), Inheritance (2011), the follow-up short story collection The Fork, the Witch, and the Worm (2018), and Murtagh (2023), the first in a follow-up duology. His first science fiction novel, To Sleep in a Sea of Stars, was published on September 15, 2020.

books.title: To Sleep in a Sea of Stars

authors.photo_attribution: Photo by DailyCoffee756 · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: TJ Klune
authors.bio: Travis John Klune is an American author of fantasy and romantic fiction featuring gay and LGBTQ+ characters. His fantasy novel The House in the Cerulean Sea is a New York Times best seller and winner of the 2021 Alex and Mythopoeic Awards. He also won the Lambda Literary Award for Gay Romance in 2014 with Into This River I Drown.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Library of Congress Life · CC0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Kaliane Bradley
authors.bio: Kaliane Bradley is an English writer and editor. Her debut novel The Ministry of Time (2024) received a number of accolades.
authors.photo_attribution: Photo by Edwardx · CC BY-SA 4.0 · via Wikimedia Commons

authors.name: Aisling Rawle
authors.bio: The Compound is a 2025 dystopian novel by Irish author Aisling Rawle. It was published by The Borough Press on 3 July 2025. It won a Goodreads Choice Award in the Readers' Favorite Science Fiction category. It is Rawle's debut novel.
